narration.essentials: A younger instruction runs while an older one is still waiting on a slow memory load.
narration.detailed: Here is the whole idea. The loop's head load `lw t2, 0(t0)` just missed in the cache — its value will not arrive for about ten cycles, at cycle 17. The `add a0, a0, t2` right behind it needs that value, so it cannot move. But `addi t1, t1, -1`, the loop counter, needs nothing from memory at all. Out-of-order issue spots that and runs it NOW, at cycle 9, while the load is still outstanding — the counter drops from 5 to 4 with the miss unresolved. Flip the toggle to in-order issue and this same decrement is pinned to cycle 20, stuck in line behind the waiting add.
narration.expert: The load occupies its load/store unit for the miss penalty and frees the issue port, so the scheduler keeps offering ready work. The dependent `add` sits in its reservation station waiting for `t2` on the common data bus; the counter's `addi` has both operands, so wakeup/select issues it around the stalled add. In-order issue refuses exactly this: it will not look past the first not-ready instruction in program order, so the ready counter waits for a load it never depended on. Same instructions, same reservation stations — only the issue policy differs, and that is the entire delta the toggle controls.

narration.essentials: The running total is a dependency chain — out-of-order issue cannot shorten it, only reach its inputs sooner.
narration.detailed: The running sum in `a0` is a chain: each `add a0, a0, t2` needs the sum before it, so out-of-order issue cannot run its links side by side — the chain's length is a floor on the runtime, which is why the speedup is only partial. What it CAN do is feed the chain sooner. With the pointer bump and counter no longer stuck behind each waiting add, the next element's load issues early and streams in on the cache's schedule instead of the reduction's. So the sum reaches 118 — the first four elements — at cycle 30 rather than cycle 42: a twelve-cycle head start that turns out to be the program's entire twelve-cycle win.
narration.expert: The reduction is a loop-carried dependency threaded through `a0`, and every link also waits on a fresh `mem-read`. Out-of-order issue cannot compress the chain itself — dependent links have no slack to run in parallel — but it decouples the LOADS that feed the chain from the chain that consumes them. In order, each iteration's load waits behind the previous `add` it never depended on; out of order, the loads stream ahead on the cache's own schedule while the adds catch up. The partial sum arriving twelve cycles earlier, cycle 30 against 42, is that decoupling made visible — and because nothing after the final load reorders, that twelve-cycle shift is exactly the program's speedup, 71 down to 59.

narration.essentials: Same total — 120 — reached in 59 cycles instead of 71.
narration.detailed: The loop is done and 120, the sum of { 5, 17, -4, 100, 2 }, is written to memory — the same total any machine reaches. Issue order never changes what a program computes, only how much waiting it can hide behind useful work. Count the cost: this out-of-order run finished in 59 cycles where in-order issue needs 71, an IPC of 0.58 against 0.48. Flip the toggle back to in-order and watch the 120 stay put while the cycle count climbs to 71.
narration.expert: 34 instructions retire either way — issue order is a throughput knob, not a correctness one, and the reorder buffer guarantees it by committing in program order no matter what order execution finished in. That is 34 / 59 = 0.58 IPC out of order against 34 / 71 = 0.48 in order. The speedup comes from letting the loop's independent work proceed during each miss, so the loads stream in ahead of the reduction that consumes them; it is sub-linear because that reduction, once fed, still cannot be overlapped with itself — a dependent chain has no internal slack for issue order to exploit. Out-of-order issue buys you the slack around a stall, never the stall's own critical path.
